seoTitle: How to Use Butler Automation in Trello
metaDescription: Set up Trello's built-in Butler automation using triggers and actions, plus custom buttons and scheduled rules, without writing code.
intro: Butler is Trello's no-code automation engine, letting you set up plain-English rules instead of manually repeating admin tasks. This guide covers finding the Automation dashboard, building your first trigger-and-action rule, and setting up scheduled routines.

steps.heading: Open the Automation dashboard
steps.body: From your board's top menu, open the centralized Automation settings workspace.

steps.heading: Understand triggers and actions
steps.body: Butler rules combine a trigger, like a card being moved, with an action, like adding a label or notifying a member.

steps.heading: Build your first rule
steps.body: Combine a specific trigger with a system response, such as moving a card and archiving it automatically.

steps.heading: Create custom buttons
steps.body: Set up card or board buttons that execute a batch of actions in a single click.

steps.heading: Schedule recurring routines
steps.body: Set up calendar-based rules, such as automatically archiving completed columns or resetting weekly lists.

steps.heading: Monitor your usage
steps.body: Keep an eye on your monthly command usage quotas so your automations keep running smoothly.

faq.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Butler?
faq.answer: No, Butler uses plain-English triggers and actions, so no coding knowledge is required.

faq.question: What's the difference between a rule and a button?
faq.answer: Rules run automatically based on a trigger, while buttons are manually clicked to execute a batch of actions on demand.

faq.question: Can Butler run on a schedule instead of a trigger?
faq.answer: Yes, you can set up calendar-based routines, like archiving completed columns weekly.

faq.question: Is there a limit to how many automations I can run?
faq.answer: Yes, Butler has monthly command usage quotas, so it's worth monitoring your usage to keep things running.
